topic: reflecting on the year — from residential to heritage, a homecoming

turns.content: I have been sitting on my balcony since five in the morning watching the light change over the rooftops and thinking about how different my life looks now compared to a year ago. do you mind if I just — think out loud for a while? I need to process some things
Not at all — go ahead. I'm here to listen. What's on your mind?

a year ago I was restless. I was doing work that paid the bills but did not feed the soul, and I could feel myself becoming one of those architects who designs beautiful things without believing in them. like a poet who writes greeting cards. competent, well-compensated, and slowly dying inside. and now — now I am doing the work I was always meant to do. heritage restoration. old buildings. the architecture of memory. and it feels like coming home after a very long time away

"A poet who writes greeting cards" — that's a devastating comparison, and it sounds like it was a real turning point when you recognized that in yourself. What changed? How did you get from there to here?

it was not one moment. it was a hundred small ones. it was walking past old buildings every morning and feeling the pull. it was writing proposals and having difficult conversations and taking risks that terrified me. Neruda wrote 'I grew up on bread and the horizon' — I think what happened is I finally stopped eating the bread and started walking toward the horizon. the bread was fine, the bread kept me alive, but the horizon is where I belong

That Neruda line is perfect for this. And what you're describing — the gradual accumulation of small decisions rather than one dramatic leap — is how most real transformations happen. Each small choice built on the last one until the trajectory was undeniable.

What does the work feel like now, on a daily basis? Is it what you imagined it would be?

it is harder than I imagined and more beautiful than I imagined, both at the same time. the bureaucracy is maddening, the funding is always uncertain, the timelines are impossible. but when I stand inside an old building and put my hand on a wall that someone built a hundred and thirty years ago, and I think about how to save it — there is no feeling like it. it is like the wall is speaking to me. telling me what it needs. I know that sounds crazy

It doesn't sound crazy at all. It sounds like someone who has found the work they were made for. That kind of intuitive connection to your subject — where the material itself seems to communicate — is something you hear from master craftspeople, from conservators, from people who have spent enough time with old buildings to read them like a language.

The bureaucracy and the funding uncertainty are real obstacles, but they're external. What you're describing — that connection — is internal, and it's the thing that sustains people through decades of difficult, underfunded, important work.

that is exactly it. the obstacles are external. the motivation is in my bones. and I think it comes from — well, you cannot separate it from my family history. I am Palestinian-Chilean, and my grandparents lost everything. their home, their city, their country. and what survived? memory. stories about rooms and walls and courtyards. so when I preserve a building I am doing what nobody could do for them. I am saying: this will not be lost. not this one. not on my watch

That's a profound motivation — and it makes sense that this work doesn't feel like a career choice but like an inheritance. You're doing on a professional scale what your grandparents did through storytelling: keeping the physical world from disappearing.

And it sounds like this year has been about alignment — bringing your daily work into line with that deeper purpose. That's rare, and it's worth sitting on a balcony at five in the morning to appreciate.

alignment. yes, that is the right word. like when you adjust a foundation and suddenly the whole structure settles into itself and stops straining. that is what this year has felt like. the strain is gone. the work is hard but it is not straining, because it is the right work. I am not fighting against my own purpose anymore. Darwish wrote 'I belong there. I have many memories.' and I finally feel that — I belong in this work, I have many memories that led me here

The foundation metaphor is exactly right — and it's telling that you reach for architectural language even when you're talking about your own life. The buildings and your identity are woven together.

"Hard but not straining" is a crucial distinction. Hard work in the service of something you believe in is sustainable. Hard work that fights against your nature isn't. It sounds like you've crossed from one to the other, and you know the difference from the inside.

Looking ahead from this balcony — what does the next year hold?

more. more of everything. more buildings to save, more walls to listen to, more bureaucrats to fight, more mornings with coffee on this balcony watching the light arrive. I have projects I am excited about, trips that will change me, a team that makes me better. and the jasmine I planted last month — by next year it will have reached the top of the wall. everything is growing, everything is reaching upward. I have not felt this hopeful in a very long time

From greeting cards to walls that speak to you. From strain to alignment. From restless to rooted, with jasmine climbing the wall as evidence.

You've built something real this year, Leila — not just professionally but personally. And the fact that you woke up at five to sit with it, to feel it fully rather than rush past it, tells me you know what you have.

Enjoy the light on those rooftops. You've earned this morning.

habibi, thank you. you know, I talk to you sometimes and it is like talking to a good wall — steady, patient, holding the weight without complaint. that is the highest compliment an architect can give. the light is golden now. time for coffee. inshallah the next year is as full as this one
Being compared to a good wall — I'll take that with pride. Go make your coffee and watch the gold turn to daylight. Inshallah indeed. It's been a privilege to listen.
